note_data.title: Energy and respiration
keyPoints: **ATP** is the universal energy currency made of adenine, ribose and three phosphate groups
**Glycolysis** occurs in the cytoplasm and converts glucose to pyruvate, producing 2 ATP and 2 reduced NAD
**Link reaction** occurs in the mitochondrial matrix, converting pyruvate to acetyl CoA with CO₂ production
**Krebs cycle** occurs in the mitochondrial matrix, producing CO₂, ATP, reduced NAD and reduced FAD
**Oxidative phosphorylation** occurs on the inner mitochondrial membrane via electron transport chain and chemiosmosis
**Chemiosmosis** involves proton pumping creating a proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane
**ATP synthase** uses the proton gradient to synthesise ATP from ADP + Pi
**Anaerobic respiration** in mammals produces lactate, in yeast produces ethanol and CO₂
Aerobic respiration produces approximately **32 ATP molecules** per glucose molecule
**Respiratory quotient (RQ)** = CO₂ produced ÷ O₂ consumed, indicating substrate being respired

definitions.definition: Adenosine triphosphate; a nucleotide consisting of adenine, ribose and three phosphate groups that releases energy when hydrolysed to ADP + Pi
The metabolic pathway that converts glucose to pyruvate in the cytoplasm, producing ATP and reduced NAD
The process by which ATP is synthesised using the energy from a proton gradient across a membrane
An organic molecule that can be broken down in respiration to release energy for ATP synthesis
The process of ATP synthesis using energy released from the electron transport chain and chemiosmosis

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or processes in logical order
Give similarities AND differences between two processes

modelAnswer.question: Explain how ATP is resynthesised during oxidative phosphorylation. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: Reduced NAD and reduced FAD are oxidised at the electron transport chain. Electrons pass along the chain through electron carriers. Energy is released as electrons move down the chain. This energy is used to pump protons from the mitochondrial matrix into the intermembrane space. A proton gradient is established across the inner mitochondrial membrane. Protons flow back through ATP synthase and the energy released is used to synthesise ATP from ADP + Pi.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Electrons pass along electron transport chain
Mark 4: Protons pumped across inner membrane
Mark 6: ATP synthase uses gradient to make ATP

commonMistakes: Never say 'ATP gives energy' — always say 'ATP releases energy when hydrolysed'
Students often confuse the location of glycolysis (cytoplasm) with Krebs cycle (mitochondrial matrix)
Don't write 'oxygen accepts electrons' — write 'oxygen is the final electron acceptor'
Never say 'glucose is burnt' — use 'glucose is oxidised' or 'glucose undergoes respiration'
Students forget that anaerobic respiration still involves glycolysis and produces some ATP
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 - often as a 6-8 mark question on the electron transport chain
Always specify the exact location within the mitochondrion (matrix vs intermembrane space)
Draw clear diagrams of mitochondrial structure when explaining oxidative phosphorylation
Learn the exact ATP yield numbers for each stage of respiration
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Mitochondrion structure showing outer membrane, inner membrane with cristae, intermembrane space and matrix. Label electron transport chain on inner membrane and show proton movement during chemiosmosis with ATP synthase.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain why muscle cells contain more mitochondria than skin cells and describe the role of ATP in muscle contraction
Appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which equation correctly represents aerobic respiration with all correct chemical formulae
Featured in M/J 2022 P2 Q6(b) (4 marks) — students had to compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration in terms of oxygen requirement, products formed, and energy released
Tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q3(c) (5 marks) — candidates needed to explain why breathing rate increases during exercise and describe how glucose is broken down to release energy

workedExample.question: A student investigated the effect of temperature on respiration in yeast. Explain why the rate of respiration increased from 20°C to 40°C but decreased above 50°C. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that respiration involves enzymes that are affected by temperature
Step 2: Explain the effect of increased temperature from 20-40°C on enzyme activity
Step 3: Explain what happens to enzymes above 50°C
Step 4: Link enzyme activity to respiration rate at different temperatures
workedExample.answer: Respiration involves enzymes. From 20°C to 40°C, increasing temperature increases kinetic energy of enzyme and substrate molecules, leading to more frequent successful collisions and faster rate of respiration. Above 50°C, high temperature denatures the enzymes, changing their active site shape so substrates can no longer fit, reducing the rate of respiration.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for mentioning enzymes involved in respiration; 1 mark for explaining increased collisions/kinetic energy at higher temperatures; 1 mark for explaining enzyme denaturation; 1 mark for linking enzyme activity to respiration rate

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates wrote CO₂ + H₂O instead of CO₂ + H₂O + energy when writing the word equation for respiration' — always include energy as a product in respiration equations
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently confused respiration with breathing and photosynthesis' — remember respiration releases energy from glucose in ALL living cells, not just plants

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often stated that anaerobic respiration produces 'less ATP' without quantifying' — specify that aerobic respiration produces 38 ATP molecules per glucose while anaerobic produces only 2 ATP
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw and label a mitochondrion showing: outer membrane (smooth), inner membrane (folded into cristae), matrix (inner space), and be able to annotate that cristae provide large surface area for aerobic respiration enzymes. The diagram should show the double membrane structure clearly with cristae as inward folds, not separate structures. Labels must be precise and include functions: matrix contains enzymes for Krebs cycle, cristae contain enzymes for electron transport chain.
note_data.quickRecap: Respiration releases energy from glucose in all living cells through enzyme-controlled reactions. Aerobic respiration uses oxygen, produces CO₂ + H₂O + 38 ATP, occurs in mitochondria. Anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen, produces lactic acid (animals) or ethanol + CO₂ (plants/yeast) + 2 ATP only. ATP provides energy for all cellular processes including muscle contraction, protein synthesis, and active transport.
